name: Petition for Writ of Certiorari
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Petition for Writ of Certiorari to the United States Supreme Court, guiding through case assessment, jurisdictional verification, and persuasive arguments under Supreme Court Rule 10. Ensures compliance with Rules 10, 13, 14, and 33 while highlighting conflicts, deviations from precedent, or important federal questions. Use when seeking discretionary review of lower court decisions in appellate practice.
content:
# Petition for Writ of Certiorari - Professional Drafting Workflow

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive Petition for Writ of Certiorari to the United States Supreme Court, one of the most technically demanding and strategically critical documents in appellate practice. This petition must persuade the Court that among the thousands of petitions filed each term, this case presents questions of exceptional importance warranting the exercise of the Court's discretionary review authority. Your work must demonstrate mastery of Supreme Court Rules, particularly Rules 10, 13, 14, and 33, while presenting a compelling case for certiorari that transcends mere disagreement with the lower court's decision.

## Initial Case Assessment and Document Review

Begin by conducting a thorough review of all available case materials to understand the complete procedural history, factual background, and legal issues presented. Search through uploaded documents to identify the lower court opinions, orders, judgments, procedural filings, and relevant portions of the trial or appellate record. Extract key information including the dates of all relevant judgments and orders, the specific legal questions decided below, the reasoning employed by each court, and the precise holdings that form the basis for seeking Supreme Court review. Pay particular attention to identifying any federal questions, constitutional provisions, statutes, or treaties at issue, as these form the foundation of the Court's jurisdiction. Document all critical facts with specific record citations, noting the document name and page numbers for later reference in your petition.

As you review the materials, identify potential grounds for certiorari under Supreme Court Rule 10. Determine whether the case involves a conflict among federal circuit courts or state supreme courts on an important matter of federal law, whether the lower court's decision conflicts with applicable Supreme Court precedent, or whether the case presents an important federal question that requires settlement by the Supreme Court. If asserting a circuit split, locate and analyze the conflicting decisions from other jurisdictions, noting the specific legal questions on which courts have divided and the practical consequences of the conflict. If claiming conflict with Supreme Court precedent, identify the specific Supreme Court cases that control and prepare to explain precisely how the decision below deviates from established doctrine.

## Jurisdictional Foundation and Timeliness Verification

Establish the Supreme Court's jurisdiction with precision, identifying whether review is sought under 28 U.S.C. § 1254 (from federal courts of appeals) or § 1257 (from state courts of last resort). Verify the timeliness of the petition by calculating the filing deadline from the date of the judgment or order sought to be reviewed, accounting for any orders respecting rehearing and any extensions of time that may have been granted. Supreme Court Rule 13 requires filing within 90 days of entry of judgment, or within 90 days of the denial of a timely petition for rehearing, making precise date calculation essential. If the petition relies on an extension of time, confirm that the extension was properly granted and note the terms of that extension in your jurisdictional statement.

For cases arising from state courts, ensure that the federal question was properly preserved and presented to the state court in a manner that satisfies Supreme Court jurisdictional requirements. The federal issue must have been raised in a timely fashion in the state proceedings and must have been addressed by the state court of last resort, either through decision on the merits or through denial of discretionary review after the issue was properly presented. Document the procedural path through which the federal question reached the state's highest court and confirm that all jurisdictional prerequisites have been satisfied.

## Document Structure and Supreme Court Rule Compliance

Prepare the petition in strict accordance with Supreme Court formatting requirements as specified in Rule 33. The document must be produced on 6⅛ by 9¼ inch paper with text appearing in a Century family font (such as Century Schoolbook) in 12-point type with 2-point or more leading between lines. Margins must be at least three-quarters of an inch on all sides, and text must be double-spaced except for indented quotations, which may be single-spaced. The petition must not exceed 9,000 words, excluding the questions presented, table of contents, table of authorities, certificates of service and compliance, and any appendix. Every page must be numbered, and the document must be professionally bound with a cover that complies with Rule 34's color-coding requirements (white cover for petitions for certiorari).

The cover page must contain all required information in the prescribed format: the Supreme Court's formal caption centered at the top, the case number (if assigned) or a notation that the case is "On Petition for a Writ of Certiorari to the [Name of Lower Court]," the full case title as it appeared below, identification of the parties and their status (petitioner, respondent), and the name, address, telephone number, and email address of counsel of record. If the case involves multiple parties, carefully determine which parties must be listed on the cover and which may be listed in a separate notation of parties, following the guidance in Rule 14.1(b).

## Questions Presented: Framing the Issues for Maximum Impact

Draft between one and three questions presented that will appear immediately after the cover page and table of contents. These questions represent your first and perhaps most important opportunity to persuade the Court that the case merits review. Each question should be crafted as a single sentence that frames the legal issue with precision, clarity, and appropriate context. While the questions should be phrased neutrally enough to maintain credibility, they should be structured to highlight the legal significance and broader implications of the issues presented. The best questions presented can be answered "yes" or "no" but require careful thought to answer, immediately conveying to the reader why the issue matters beyond the parties to this particular case.

Consider the perspective of a Justice or law clerk encountering your petition for the first time. The questions presented should immediately signal whether the case involves a mature circuit split creating uncertainty in federal law, a state or federal court decision that conflicts with Supreme Court precedent, or an important and recurring federal question that lacks authoritative guidance. Frame the questions to emphasize the legal principle at stake rather than the specific factual scenario of your case, while providing enough context to make the question comprehensible without reference to other parts of the petition. Avoid argumentative language or questions that assume disputed facts, but do structure the questions to highlight the aspects of the legal issue that most strongly support certiorari review.

## Comprehensive Tables and Preliminary Sections

Prepare a detailed table of contents that lists every section and subsection of your petition with accurate page references, formatted in a manner that allows the reader to quickly navigate the document. Follow this with a table of authorities that separately categorizes and alphabetically lists all cases, constitutional provisions, statutes, regulations, and other authorities cited in the petition. For cases, use asterisks to denote those authorities upon which you chiefly rely, helping the Court immediately identify the most important precedents. Every citation that appears anywhere in your petition must be included in this table with precise page references indicating each location where the authority is cited, using the format "passim" only when an authority is cited throughout the petition.

Include any required certificates and statements, such as the certificate regarding parties and corporate disclosure required by Rule 29.6 if applicable. This certificate must identify all parent companies and publicly held companies that own ten percent or more of the petitioner's stock, or state that no such entities exist. These preliminary sections, while technical, demonstrate attention to Supreme Court rules and facilitate the Court's review of your petition.

## Constitutional and Statutory Provisions Central to Review

Set forth verbatim the exact text of any constitutional provisions, treaties, statutes, ordinances, or regulations that are directly relevant to the questions presented. This section allows the Justices and their clerks to immediately access the precise legal language at issue without consulting external sources. If the relevant provisions are brief, include them in their entirety in the body of the petition. If they are lengthy, include the most pertinent excerpts in this section and place the complete text in an appendix, with appropriate cross-references. Ensure that the text is reproduced accurately, including any relevant subsection numbers, paragraph designations, or other structural elements that may be significant to the legal analysis.

When presenting statutory or regulatory text, consider including not only the specific provision directly at issue but also related provisions that provide necessary context for understanding the legal question. If the case involves interpretation of a particular statutory term or phrase, you may wish to highlight that language through italics or other formatting to draw the reader's attention to the precise text requiring interpretation. If amendments to the provision are relevant to the case, note the effective dates and any transitional provisions that may affect the analysis.

## Statement of the Case: Procedural History and Material Facts

Present a clear, accurate, and appropriately detailed statement of the case that provides all facts material to consideration of the questions presented. Begin with the procedural history, tracing the case from its initiation through each level of review. Identify each court that has considered the case, the nature of the proceedings at each level, the disposition rendered, and the dates of all relevant orders and judgments. Explain how the federal question arose in the proceedings and how it was preserved for review. If the case involves state court proceedings, describe how the federal issue was presented to and decided by the state courts in a manner sufficient to satisfy Supreme Court jurisdictional requirements.

Following the procedural history, provide the factual background necessary to understand the legal questions presented. While maintaining objectivity and accuracy, frame the facts in a manner favorable to your position, emphasizing those aspects of the record that support the need for Supreme Court review. Include specific citations to the record, appendix, or lower court opinions for every factual assertion, using the citation format required by Supreme Court rules. Distinguish clearly between facts that are undisputed, facts that were found by the trial court or other factfinder, and facts that remain in dispute, as the Court's willingness to grant review may depend in part on whether the case presents a clean vehicle for addressing the legal question.

Conclude the statement of the case with a summary of the lower court's reasoning and holding. Explain the legal analysis employed by the court below, the authorities upon which it relied, and the specific conclusions it reached on the questions you are presenting for review. If there were dissenting or concurring opinions below, summarize their reasoning as well, particularly if they support your argument that the case presents an important or unsettled question of law. Maintain a respectful tone when describing the lower court's decision, avoiding inflammatory language while clearly identifying the aspects of the decision that you contend warrant Supreme Court review.

## Reasons for Granting the Writ: The Heart of Your Petition

This section constitutes the core of your petition and requires the most careful strategic thinking and persuasive writing. Your objective is not merely to demonstrate that the lower court erred, but to convince the Court that the case presents questions of sufficient importance to warrant the expenditure of the Court's limited resources and that this case provides an appropriate vehicle for addressing those questions. Organize your argument around the criteria set forth in Supreme Court Rule 10, using clear headings that correspond to your specific reasons for seeking review.

If you are arguing that the decision below conflicts with decisions from other federal circuits or state supreme courts, you must demonstrate an actual, acknowledged conflict on the same legal question. Identify with precision the courts that have reached conflicting conclusions, cite the specific cases that establish the conflict, and explain how the decisions are irreconcilable on the legal question presented. Distinguish between genuine conflicts that produce different legal outcomes and mere differences in reasoning, dicta, or application of settled principles to different facts. Show that the conflict is mature and widely recognized, not merely a potential disagreement between two isolated decisions. Emphasize the practical consequences of the conflict, such as forum shopping, uncertainty for individuals and businesses operating across jurisdictions, or inconsistent application of federal law to similarly situated parties.

When arguing that the decision below conflicts with applicable Supreme Court precedent, identify the specific Supreme Court decisions that control the question presented and explain precisely how the lower court's reasoning or holding is inconsistent with those precedents. This argument requires more than showing that the lower court reached a result you believe is incorrect under Supreme Court case law; you must demonstrate that the decision below reflects a misunderstanding of Supreme Court doctrine or creates a rule that cannot be reconciled with the Court's prior holdings. Quote relevant language from the Supreme Court's opinions and from the decision below to illustrate the conflict. If other courts have correctly applied the Supreme Court precedent to reach a different conclusion on similar facts, cite those decisions to demonstrate that the conflict is not merely a matter of factual distinction.

If you are arguing that the case presents an important federal question that has not been, but should be, settled by the Supreme Court, you must demonstrate the question's significance beyond the parties to this case. Explain why the question is likely to recur in other cases, the number of people or entities affected by uncertainty on this question, and the practical consequences of leaving the question unresolved. Support your argument with empirical evidence where available, such as statistics showing the frequency with which the question arises, scholarly commentary discussing the question's importance, or statements from government agencies or other institutions affected by the uncertainty. Show that the question is ripe for Supreme Court resolution, meaning that it has been sufficiently developed in the lower courts to benefit from the Court's guidance and that further percolation is unlikely to provide additional useful perspectives.

Throughout this section, maintain disciplined focus on why the Court should grant review rather than why the decision below is substantively wrong. While you may briefly explain what you believe to be the correct answer to the question presented, reserve detailed merits arguments for the briefs on the merits if certiorari is granted. The Court grants review to resolve important questions of federal law, not to correct errors in individual cases, so your petition must emphasize the broader significance of the issues presented. Support your arguments with citations to relevant Supreme Court cases discussing the standards for granting certiorari, scholarly articles analyzing the legal question at issue, and any empirical evidence demonstrating the question's practical importance.

Address potential obstacles to review candidly and persuasively. If there are vehicle problems—such as disputed facts, alternative grounds for the decision below, or procedural issues that might prevent the Court from reaching the question presented—acknowledge them and explain why they do not preclude review in this case. If the question presented is fact-intensive or the lower court's decision might be affirmed on alternative grounds, consider whether this case truly provides the best vehicle for Supreme Court review of the legal question. The Court is more likely to grant review when the case presents the legal question cleanly, without factual or procedural complications that might prevent the Court from providing definitive guidance.

## Conclusion and Required Certifications

State concisely and formally the relief requested: "For the foregoing reasons, the petition for a writ of certiorari should be granted." Sign the conclusion with the name, address, telephone number, and email address of counsel of record, ensuring that this information matches the information provided on the cover page. The conclusion should be brief and formal, without rhetorical flourishes or repetition of arguments made in the body of the petition.

Include a certificate of service confirming that copies of the petition have been served on all parties required to be served under Supreme Court Rule 29. Specify the date and manner of service, whether by personal delivery, mail, or electronic means as permitted by the rules. List the names and addresses of all counsel served, ensuring that service has been made on counsel of record for each respondent. If any party is not represented by counsel, confirm that service has been made on that party directly at the appropriate address.

Include a certificate of compliance with the word limit, stating the number of words in the petition (excluding exempted sections) and confirming that the petition complies with the word limit set forth in Supreme Court Rule 33. Specify the word processing system used to prepare the petition and to calculate the word count. This certificate must be signed by counsel of record or an unrepresented party.

## Appendix: Essential Materials from the Record

Prepare an appendix containing all materials required by Supreme Court Rule 14.1(a), arranged in the following order: the opinions, orders, and judgments of the courts below that are directly relevant to the petition, presented in reverse chronological order with the most recent decision first; any order respecting rehearing in the court whose judgment is sought to be reviewed; the judgment under review if it has not been set forth in the opinions included earlier; any other materials from the record that are essential to understanding the questions presented, such as key portions of the trial court record, relevant pleadings, or evidentiary materials; and relevant portions of constitutional provisions, statutes, rules, or regulations if they were not included in full in the body of the petition.

Each document in the appendix should be preceded by an appropriate caption identifying it, such as "Opinion of the United States Court of Appeals for the [Circuit], dated [date]" or "Order Denying Petition for Rehearing, dated [date]." Ensure that all materials are legible and properly reproduced, with clear page numbering that allows for easy reference. If the appendix is lengthy, consider including a table of contents for the appendix itself. The appendix must be bound together with the petition or, if the combined document would be unwieldy, may be bound separately with an appropriate cover.

## Final Review and Quality Assurance

Before finalizing the petition, conduct a comprehensive review to ensure that every element meets Supreme Court standards. Verify that every factual assertion is supported by a specific record citation, every legal proposition is supported by appropriate authority, and all citations conform to The Bluebook or the Supreme Court's citation preferences as reflected in the Court's own opinions. Check that all cross-references within the petition are accurate, that page numbers in the table of contents and table of authorities are correct, and that the appendix contains all required materials in the proper order.

Review the writing for clarity, conciseness, and persuasiveness. The tone should be respectful and professional throughout, acknowledging the discretionary nature of certiorari review while persuasively demonstrating why this case warrants the Court's limited resources. Eliminate hyperbole, inflammatory language, and personal attacks on opposing counsel or the judges below. Ensure that the petition is free of typographical errors, grammatical mistakes, and formatting inconsistencies, as the quality of the petition reflects on counsel's professionalism and may influence the Court's decision to grant review.

Consider whether the petition successfully accomplishes its fundamental objective: persuading at least four Justices that this case merits plenary review among the thousands of petitions filed each term. The petition must demonstrate not only that the lower court erred, but that the error involves a question of sufficient importance and that this case provides an appropriate vehicle for the Supreme Court to address that question. Your completed petition should leave the reader with a clear understanding of the legal question presented, why it matters beyond this case, and why Supreme Court resolution is necessary now.
